title: Lineage Tree and Story Focus
problem: The feature list shows all entries in a flat-ish tree, but visitors have no way to understand parent/child relationships visually or to skip sub-stories they don't care about. A reader who only wants the top-level decisions is forced to scroll past every spawned child, and a reader who wants the full thread has no clear visual guide for following it.
analysis: Parent features act as chapter headings — their children are the supporting detail. A collapse affordance on any parent lets readers hide its entire sub-tree in one click. The collapsed state lives in React component state (not URL), so it resets on reload — intentional, since the default reading experience should show everything. Visual connectors (an indented left border or subtle tree line) make the parent/child relationship legible without needing labels. A collapsed parent shows a child-count badge so the reader knows what they're skipping.

decisions.decision: Collapse entire sub-tree when a parent is collapsed, not just direct children
decisions.rationale: A reader skipping a story wants to skip the whole thread, not just one level. Collapsing only direct children forces repeated clicks and leaves grandchildren visible.
alternativesConsidered: Collapse one level at a time — more granular but tedious for deep trees
Hide button per card — too granular, no sense of the thread being skipped

decisions.decision: Ephemeral collapse state (component state, not URL or localStorage)
decisions.rationale: The default experience should always show the full story. Persisting collapsed state would hide content from returning visitors without them realising it.
alternativesConsidered: localStorage — persists across sessions, risks permanently hiding content
URL params — shareable but adds complexity for a simple reading preference

decisions.rationale: Readers need to know what they're skipping. A badge like '▸ 3' makes the collapse feel intentional rather than like missing content.
alternativesConsidered: No badge — ambiguous, reader may think the tree is just shallow
Expand/collapse all global toggle — useful addition later, too broad for v1

decisions.decision: Collapsed state hides child cards only — the parent card always shows its full content (revised by feat-2026-016)
decisions.rationale: Originally the card itself minimised to title-only when collapsed. feat-2026-016 removed this: the card body (problem, tags, spawn-reason) is always visible. Minimising the card confused readers who expected to see what they were skipping past.
alternativesConsidered: Hide problem/tags/spawn-reason on collapsed parent (original behaviour) — removed, made collapsed cards feel like broken stubs

lineage.spawnReason: The design system established visual tokens; now the tree structure needs interaction design — collapse/expand behaviour and lineage connectors are a natural next layer on top of the existing card layout.

knownLimitations: Collapse state resets on reload — deep readers who carefully curated their view lose it on refresh
No keyboard shortcut to collapse/expand a sub-tree — requires mouse click on the toggle
Child-count badge only counts direct children, not the full recursive depth of the hidden sub-tree
successCriteria: Any feature with children shows a collapse toggle. Clicking it hides the entire sub-tree and shows a badge with the number of hidden stories. Clicking again restores them. Features with no children show no toggle.
implementation: Collapse state is a Set<string> of collapsed featureKeys held in App component state, initialised on mount with every key that has children (so the tree starts fully collapsed — see feat-2026-016). On each render, visibleTree walks the full tree and checks whether any ancestor of each node is in collapsedKeys — if so, the node is omitted from the rendered list. The collapse toggle (▾ / ▸ N) lives in the .card-gutter div to the left of the card (feat-2026-016), and appears on any FeatureCard whose descendantCount > 0. Toggling adds or removes the key from collapsedKeys. The card itself always renders its full content (title, problem, tags) regardless of collapsed state — collapse only controls child visibility, not the card body.
